title: Fondant draping
subtitle: Rolling and laying sugarpaste over a cake
excerpt: How to roll ready-made sugarpaste to an even sheet, lift it over a crumb-coated cake, and smooth it down to a wrinkle-free finish. The technique that gives celebration cakes a flat, polished surface for painting and decoration.
sourceNotes: Rolled sugarpaste (fondant icing) as a cake covering became standard in British celebration cake decoration through the mid-twentieth century. The technique of rolling a thin sheet and draping it over a ganache or marzipan-coated cake is described in domestic cake-decorating guides from the 1960s and 1970s and is now the dominant method for wedding and tiered cakes.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: A fondant-covered cake keeps at room temperature for 3-5 days. Do not refrigerate: condensation forms on the fondant surface as it warms back up.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pliable, dough-like icing made from icing sugar, glucose syrup, and gelatin (or a vegetable alternative). Also called ready-to-roll fondant or rolled fondant. It is rolled to a thin sheet and draped over cakes or moulded into decorative shapes. Sets firm at room temperature. Available ready-made in blocks in most supermarkets.

content.text: over a cake requires a prepared surface, a sheet rolled to even thickness, and enough drape to cover the cake sides plus the board. The cake beneath needs to be sticky enough to hold the fondant: a coat of ganache or buttercream provides this. Marzipan is used on fruit cakes. Fondant applied to a dry or crumby surface will not stick and will pull away.

Knead the sugarpaste until it is warm and smooth before rolling. Cold fondant cracks and tears when draped. If the fondant is very stiff, microwave it in 5-second bursts until pliable. Dust the rolling surface with icing sugar, not cornflour: cornflour can cause fermentation spots under the fondant over time.

Dust the work surface lightly with icing sugar. Knead the sugarpaste until smooth. Roll out to a circle approximately 5 mm thick and large enough to cover the top and sides of the cake plus 5 cm extra: for a 20 cm round cake with 7 cm sides, roll to at least 44 cm across. Lift and rotate the fondant a quarter turn every few passes to keep an even circle and check it isn't sticking.

Roll the fondant loosely around the rolling pin to lift it. Unroll it over the cake, centring it on the top. Smooth the top surface with the palm of your hand in broad, flat strokes, working out any bubbles toward the edges. Use an icing smoother for a flat, even finish on the top.

Working around the cake, gently ease the fondant down the sides, lifting and pleating as needed to prevent tucks or folds. Smooth the sides in downward strokes with the icing smoother, working from the top edge toward the base. The fondant should lie flat against the cake without air pockets. Trim the excess at the base with a sharp knife held flat against the board. Leave to dry at room temperature for at least 1 hour before adding any decoration.
